card.intuition: A consistency model used in distributed systems where, if no new updates are made to a data item, all accesses to that item will eventually return the last updated value.
card.analogy: Social media likes: if you like a post, your friend across the world might not see it for a few seconds, but after a short wait, everyone will see the same total count.
when_to_use: High-scale systems where latency is more critical than instant accuracy (e.g., DNS, global social networks)
When using Amazon S3, DynamoDB, or Cassandra in certain configurations
Reducing network bottlenecks by allowing data to propagate asynchronously
gotchas: It makes the 'Read-Your-Own-Writes' experience difficult without session-stickiness or extra client-side logic
Not suitable for financial transactions where a $0 balance must be reflected instantly worldwide

steps.label: Write
steps.annotation: Write committed to primary node. Immediately visible there.

steps.label: Replicate
steps.annotation: Change asynchronously propagated to replica nodes — lag exists.

steps.label: Stale Read
steps.annotation: Read on replica may return old value during propagation window.

steps.label: Converge
steps.annotation: Given no new writes, all nodes eventually converge to same value.

steps.label: Read-your-writes
steps.annotation: Strategy: route user reads to primary or track vector clock.

exercise.prompt: Identify the term for achieving the exact same data across all nodes at the exact same time (the opposite of eventual consistency).
exercise.starter_code: In a distributed system, requiring all nodes to see the same data at all times is called ______ ___________.
hints: It is the 'Strong' version of consistency
